William Friedkin keeps it grim and intimate as a lonely motel setting and a desperate relationship turn into a sealed chamber of delusion. It’s a harsher, more theatrical descent, but the same attraction to minds slipping past the edge of reality.

Red Rooms::My House Walkthrough.primary_angle: Internet-era obsession horror that turns interior space into a psychological trap
supporting_points: shared fixation on blurred reality and private ritual
horror built from domestic space rather than overt spectacle
matches the source film’s morbid, inward-turning psychology
Red Rooms::My House Walkthrough.blurb: A tight adjacent pick for the same kind of unnerving descent: less procedural than psychological, and more haunted by private obsession than by plot. The appeal is the way ordinary space becomes a chamber of fixation, echoing Red Rooms’ interest in reality slipping under the pressure of obsession. If you were drawn to that film’s sealed-off, feverish atmosphere, this points in a similarly intimate and disturbing direction.

Anora::The Florida Project.primary_angle: Sean Baker’s human-scale empathy, traded from adult chaos to childhood wonder
supporting_points: marginal lives in vivid urban settings
Anora::The Florida Project.blurb: If Anora is Baker turning a fast, bruised Cinderella story into romantic chaos, The Florida Project is the quieter, sadder counterpoint: another portrait of people on the edges, but seen through a child’s wide-open gaze. Both films are drawn to the beauty and instability of precarious lives, and both find tenderness inside rough surroundings. The shift from Brooklyn nightlife to a Florida motel gives the pairing a sharp contrast in tone without losing Baker’s compassionate eye.

Anora::After Hours.primary_angle: A downtown New York spiral of bad luck and nocturnal absurdity, with the same city-as-maze energy that drives Anora.
supporting_points: New York City as the central pressure cooker
Anora::After Hours.blurb: After Hours is the cleaner, meaner cousin to Anora’s New York chaos: another impulsive night that turns romance into a downtown ordeal. Where Anora moves from Cinderella wish-fulfillment into social warfare, Scorsese’s film traps its protagonist in a surreal city labyrinth of dates, subways, nightclubs, and escalating bad luck. If you liked Sean Baker’s appetite for nocturnal momentum and comic panic, this is the more feverish, prickly variation.

Häxan::Freaks.primary_angle: A natural next stop if you want early horror that treats the grotesque as both spectacle and social critique.
supporting_points: silent-era horror with a carnival-of-the-outcast sensibility
darkly comic use of bodies, superstition, and shock
pairs well with Häxan’s mix of horror and unsettling humor
Häxan::Freaks.blurb: If Häxan’s witches-and-hysteria mash-up worked for you, Freaks is the cleaner, more humanly cruel companion piece. Tod Browning turns the sideshow into a moral trap, building horror from bodies, desire, and revenge rather than gothic pageantry. It has the same old-world unease and grotesque humor, but trades medieval sabotage for circus intimacy.

Häxan::Night of the Hunter.primary_angle: A good next stop if what you want from Häxan is witchy dread filtered through religious hypocrisy and theatrical menace.
Häxan::Night of the Hunter.blurb: If Häxan’s appeal for you is less its historical argument than its taste for blasphemy, accusation, and occult unease, Night of the Hunter sits nearby. It trades medieval witchcraft for a false preacher and a family trapped in his orbit, but keeps the same fascination with religious performance turning sinister. It’s a sharper, more modern kind of menace, yet still rooted in gothic dread.

Possibly in Michigan::We're All Going to.primary_angle: A drift from analog body-horror weirdness into digital isolation and identity horror.
supporting_points: female-centered menace and reversal of victim/aggressor roles
from mall stalking to bedroom/internet dread
Possibly in Michigan::We're All Going to.blurb: If Possibly in Michigan feels like horror sung through a dream of consumer spaces, We’re All Going to the World’s Fair carries that same unease into the glow of the laptop screen. Both films center women navigating threat, performance, and the unstable line between fear and agency, but Schoenbrun’s film swaps the mall’s grotesque physicality for internet solitude and identity blur. It’s a fitting next stop if you want your surreal horror colder, quieter, and more inwardly unnerving.

Wax, or The Discovery of Television Amon.blurb: If Wax turns television into a psychedelic implant and lets the resulting hallucinations take over, We're All Going to the World's Fair updates that same unease for the internet age. Both films linger in the liminal zone where a private obsession starts rewriting reality, with isolated bedrooms, altered perception, and a creeping sense that the medium itself is contagious. Schoenbrun’s film is the colder, more contemporary echo of Blair’s fever dream.

Summer Palace::Ash is Purest White.primary_angle: A natural next step if you want Chinese cinema where romance collides with history and social upheaval
supporting_points: love story strained by violence and public change
from campus intimacy to criminal underworld displacement
Summer Palace::Ash is Purest White.blurb: If Summer Palace’s mix of private desire and national rupture stayed with you, Ash Is Purest White carries that same charge into a very different register. Jia Zhangke turns a love story into a long aftermath of loyalty, betrayal, and survival, with China’s criminal underworld replacing Lou Ye’s campus unrest. It’s less feverish on the surface, but just as interested in how history keeps rearranging intimacy.

Summer Palace::Raise the Red Lanter.primary_angle: A powerful follow-up for viewers drawn to women trapped inside rigid Chinese power structures, where desire becomes inseparable from social pressure.
supporting_points: both center young women entering oppressive new worlds
romance collides with control, rivalry, and punishment
moves from campus unrest to a more formal, period-bound pressure cooker
Summer Palace::Raise the Red Lanter.blurb: If Summer Palace hit hardest for the way private longing gets crushed by larger systems, Raise the Red Lantern pushes that logic into an even colder architecture of control. Zhang Yimou turns a wealthy household into a battlefield of ritual, rivalry, and surveillance, where every favor has a cost. It’s less volatile than Lou Ye’s modern tragedy, but just as ruthless about how desire is managed and punished.
